content.problemStatement:
Ram and Shyam are sitting next to each other, hoping to cheat on an exam. However, the examination board has prepared ppp different sets of questions (numbered 000 through p−1p-1p−1), which will be distributed to the students in the following way:


The students are assigned roll numbers — pairwise distinct positive integers.
If a student's roll number is rrr, this student gets the ((r−1)%p)((r-1)\%p)((r−1)%p)-th set of questions.
Obviously, Ram and Shyam can cheat only if they get the same set of questions.

You are given the roll numbers of Ram and Shyam: AAA and BBB respectively. Find the number of values of ppp for which they can cheat, or determine that there is an infinite number of such values.
constraints: A, B are pairwise distinct positive integers
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T. Each test case contains two space-separated integers AAA and BBB.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line — the number of values of ppp for which Ram and Shyam can cheat, or −1 if there is an infinite number of such values.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Example case 1: They can cheat for p=1, p=2 or p=4.
Explanation: Example case 2: They can cheat for p=3 or p=7.
Explanation: Example case 3: They can cheat for p=5.
Explanation: Example case 4: They cannot cheat.
Explanation: Example case 5: They can cheat for p=1.

solution.approach: For each test case, we need to find the number of values of ppp for which Ram and Shyam can cheat. We can do this by finding the GCD of (A-1) and (B-1). If the GCD is 1, then there are no values of ppp for which Ram and Shyam can cheat. If the GCD is not 1, then we need to find the LCM of (A-1) and (B-1) and check if it is equal to (A-1) or (B-1). If it is equal to (A-1) or (B-1), then there is only one value of ppp for which Ram and Shyam can cheat. If the LCM is not equal to (A-1) or (B-1), then there are two values of ppp for which Ram and Shyam can cheat.
